title: A systematic review and Bayesian meta-analysis of co-offending characteristics
methods: Bayesian meta-regression (crime type, sex, age group)
data_sources: Official police and court records (via primary studies)

data_scope.publication_period: no restriction; searches conducted July 2023 and updated January 2025
data_scope.geographic_coverage: predominantly Global North: USA, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand; limited China, Colombia, Turkey

core_conclusion: Across 110 studies from 17 countries (approx. 46 million offences and 3.5 million offenders), 29% of offences and 50% of offenders involved co-offending, with an average group size of 2.73. Property crimes show higher co-offence prevalence driven by practical collaboration needs; violent crimes exhibit larger group sizes as a by-product of group dynamics. Young offenders co-offend at rates 30 percentage points higher than adults and in larger groups. Male and female offenders co-offend at similar overall rates, but among violent offenders females co-offend at higher rates than males. Inconsistent reporting across studies impedes cross-study comparison, and the authors call for standardized co-offending metrics.
why_it_matters: First global quantitative synthesis of co-offending covering ~46M offences and ~3.5M offenders; challenges incarceration-only prevention strategies; highlights age and crime-type nuances for targeted intervention; provides concrete reporting recommendations for the field; is fully open-source and preregistered.
why_nature: Cross-disciplinary synthesis bridging criminology, sociology, and behavioural science; addresses a fundamental human-behaviour question (why people commit crimes together); applies innovative Bayesian hierarchical methods to social science; directly informs criminal justice policy.
added_value_to_my_research: Bayesian co-offending posteriors as calibrated priors for spatially-varying crime-surface models (MGWR/KDE)
Posterior predictive distribution for group size calibrates agent-based co-offending ABM parameters
Study-level moderators enable explainable-ML meta-regression (XGBoost + SHAP) to rank reporting-design effects
Urban scaling extension: test whether co-offending prevalence scales superlinearly with city population
Co-offending network topology + mobile-phone mobility flows -> SIR-style criminal contagion diffusion model

related.relation: governance-type organized crime and group dynamics in a UK city — contextualizes co-offending group structures

related.title: Urban scaling of firearm violence, ownership and accessibility in the United States
related.relation: urban-scale analysis of crime — enables cross-linking of co-offending rates with city-population scaling

related.title: Violence, city size and geographical isolation in African cities
related.relation: urban scaling of violence including smaller and isolated cities — extends global coverage beyond the paper's English-speaking country bias

related.title: Acute mental health benefits of urban nature
related.relation: systematic review and meta-analysis methodology in urban social science — methodological parallel for co-offending synthesis

Results of the meta-regression models: fixed effects of crime type, sex, and age group on co-offence and co-offender prevalence
